title: Multi Agent Top Down Analysis
author: fibspeak
selftext:
In recent posts I've shown you some of my execution and results tracking stuff. 

Now we will look at how we are setting full top down analysis. 

  
I dont want to have simple tools just trading when a MA crosses something and RSI is something else. 

The goal of the multi agent system is to be able to emulate step by step my decision making process based on 20 yrs of experience, and maybe even make it more efficient. 

Tricky to do because my process is multi step and contingent.  I'll start with a main thesis, such as the 100 price level will be important. This is a firm assumption the whole plan will be built around. 100 will be important and if it holds it will look like (hold path1, 2 or 3) and if it breaks it will look like (break path 1,2 or 3).  Each of these paths map to a trading strategy. For example, break path 1 might use a basic breakout and retest strategy.  Hold 2 might use a head and shoulders strategy. 

The process is one overall plan on something that is important.  Branches into two main paths of bull or bear decision and then each of these spin off into their sub branches. We go from broad plan, to directional plan, to executable strategies and then standard follow ups (path 1 failing a retest may trigger the use of contingency plan 1). 

I've done this process so long I do it effortlessly now manually. Its just a simple process of what do I believe (100 is important price), what is my bias (i think i'll fade it) and what is my counter plans (how the bear trade fails and a bull trade forms).  Then I have a family of different strategies I've developed over the years and knowing which one to fit into which move is as natural as putting a shoe on the right foot. 

  
But this is tricky to fully automate. 

  
The decision trees are simple. Tedious, but simple. It's a lot of work defining where things are coupled, how they fail, what a failure means, what happens next if they succeed and the known risks and caveats inside of the different assumptions.  But its doable. You can just skill spill into a LLM for a while and get it to organise that into something coherent. 

What's more difficult is getting a stateless LLM to do what is by its very nature stateful tracking. A trade plan than is done top down and transitioning from one phase to another with different rules for how to act in each one is a state machine. The LLM is stateless.  The trade plan is dependent on continuity but the LLM does not have continuity. 

Every single API call you make to a LLM you are invoking a blabbering idiot that knows nothing about your method, the state of the current plan or what you want to do next.  It's going to be by default inclined to say generic stuff and if you're allowing it to write in prose it will fall back into massive ambiguity.  Clean and actionable trade forecasts are not common in the LLM training set.  Word salads from people who're paid to write about trades they will not make are. 

  
As well as the backend technical benefits of this, I also want to be able to read it. To check its right and to share it with other people (for a small fee, all this costs money to do). 

So here's how I did it; 

  
1 - Research.  I collated lots and lot of data on historic booms and busts. I already had a lot of technical understanding of this and I also gathered a lot of other data also. Now I have a common source of truth for historic booms and busts. These are durable facts. The PE ratios of things at highs/lows. What technical patterns worked and failed in different booms/busts etc. Its all objective and just deals with hard facts or perceived facts (ta always has some subjectivity). 

  
2 - LLM personas access the research and then begin to write thesis'.  For now my personas are writing thesis' around things I think about (much easier for testing) but they will also be able to make up their own ideas and posting series in the future. In the current implementation I give them a persona and a thesis they are working on. 

  
For example, "Macro mind" is a neutral facts based analyst. They will provide the full thesis for bull/bear plans on their topic of discussion. No bias or preference, it just goes deep into the whole thesis. For example, maybe im interested in whether AI stocks have the market in a 1996 or 2000 situation. 

https://preview.redd.it/yr6n4rl8ag3h1.png?width=1393&format=png&auto=webp&s=5628f1d78ee5b7f04561fb7a6f9615156e3230c9

Here's the intro for the base post about that.

We can also insert images along with some TA directly into the posts (but how i did this bit is at least another post, maybe 2 or 3). 

https://preview.redd.it/25p93bciag3h1.png?width=1403&format=png&auto=webp&s=1804c9d39478e5cd10cf7de7d70acd753a5aabb1

 

It can come up with different points of interest to support either end. 

https://preview.redd.it/nlu82pkqag3h1.png?width=1348&format=png&auto=webp&s=0dee20bd76816805e60f3e21a1c0c351e0684d12

  
And then we have the gem in our solving of the state problem for LLMs. We can then split these into different bullish and bearish branches for deep dives and progressively smaller charts used. Through this process an interesting macro thesis is made. Then some shape it added to it with SR levels from bigger charts. Then different types of plans at important levels are made. These are then mapped to setups/strategies and now we have went from a very high level vague idea to exact defined  actionable decision tree. 

https://preview.redd.it/c19jezkbbg3h1.png?width=1402&format=png&auto=webp&s=58babe0ec2514afe0e6959b5007b98db982cfc27

  
Every post has a chain of custody. The LLM isnt now being invoked into an empty space with some light prompts to "win market make no mistakes".  The LLM lands inside this step by step explanation of what we are doing. Specifically what branch we are in now (this makes it a state machine) and the specific rules that end that branch. If applicable, what we do when that branch ends. 

  
By the time we get to the "execution" branches and actual trade signals are appearing its possible to trace the idea back to its very inception seeing all the points at which the bias forked from bull to bear. See if the overall plan is profitable or not so far, understand what the plan will do next if the primary expectations fail. 

Rather than the alert being "the 200 SMA is crossing the 100 and that RSI thing you like is happening" we have a structure that goes like; 

\> Macro thesis (sometimes entirely fundamental). Its broad. Maybe interesting. Useless for trading. 

\> Bull and bear plans off a macro thesis. TA done on weekly / monthly charts. its useful but there is also a lot of time and noise. doesnt help for short term decisions. 

\> Meta plans inside of these bull bear plans. Coming from 4 hour charts or so and viable for swing trading. 

\> Fine touch execution. 1 hour and below timeframes to take actual entries and stops using pre defined strategy rules that have tested edges and perform well in that type of market the branch analysis assumes. 

  
Through these a LLM agent can express a top down trade plan in newsletter type format (grounded in research and domain knowledge) and as with all the other tools I've shown you these can format their trade plans as payloads to broker's APIs so we can do the real trade execution on this at the same time as the content creation.

comments.author: InnerGarage4519
comments.body: It’s so funny when someone says they have 20 years of experience and it boils down to the most saturated concepts in TA

replies.author: fibspeak
replies.body: i have 20 yrs experience in one thing that works. if you want something fancier, look elsewhere.  i stopped when i found something that worked. then tripled down on it. yr on yr.

replies.author: InnerGarage4519
replies.body: If it works, what’s stops it from decaying? Especially if it’s something as simple as generic TA

replies.author: fibspeak
replies.body:
it may decay but after 20 yrs of doing it manually i programmed them with professional metrics tracking and then showed about 100 trades with a brier ranking of close to 0. Which mean I my assumptions almost perfectly fitted my results.  if it decays, i may adjust or leave the market. Small stop hunt evolutions have already happened but they didnt break the edge, just moved the entries a bit. 

my results show trading at 1:3 RR hitting almost the exat predicted win rate. isnt easy to do. 

  
i do not claim to know for sure what the future will be. none of us know. we all bet on what we think is best.

replies.author: fibspeak
replies.body:
for what its worth, ive worked in this specific space for 20 yrs and never once seen anyone propose a 6.8 extension as a bubble decision point. not even once. if this is "generic" i must be hanging out in the wrong places. 

i'll share some of my research on how i came up with this from knowledge proprietary idea in other posts. it will take me weeks to explain all the work ive done to set this up.
